title: Stop pretending billionaires built the future
content_html:
This essay is part of my book We Should Own The Economy, which I am writing in public. Readers can still back the project and earn a share of the profits when it sells 👇🏻

Learn More

America’s billionaires will call anything “socialism”—so long as it keeps them from paying taxes.

They parrot the idea that we need billionaires, because it’s the promise of million-dollar payouts that incentivizes them to build the internet, autonomous vehicles, and rockets.

“You want to avoid living in a Soviet hellscape?” Rainmaker CEO Augustus Doricko recently asked founders on X. “Work fucking harder. Stop fucking around with amounts of money for which most Americans would fall on their knees and cry to God with thanks. Proactively ensure everyone in the nation reaps a portion of the reward of your labor, or they’re going to take it from you.”

If founders were just more grateful for their money and worked harder for it, he says, rather than flexing their exuberant lifestyles, they wouldn’t attract the ire of the middle class who earn 1000x less, will never afford a house, and are now garnering righteous indignation against their corporate founders and calling to “tax the rich.”

Doricko admires the “old capitalists” who worked hard and built public works, instead. If only founders were like them, we wouldn’t be calling for “socialism.”

This is, respectfully, ridiculous. The Carnegies and Rockefellers and Morgans earned more than 50,000x the average worker. They may have installed beautiful public libraries and universities, but workers weren’t grateful—they striked and protested. Outrage culminated in the Fair Labor Standards Act: Capitalists were forced to pay employees a minimum wage in 1938, and the top marginal tax rate was increased to 94% by the 1940s.

It worked—big time.

Capitalists were forced to pay workers more, so they necessarily earned less. To avoid paying high income taxes, they invested more money in their companies and workers. Workers earned more, and could afford to buy what those companies produced. From the 1940s to the 1970s, the US was the most economically equal it had ever been. The share of income going to the top 1% fell to historic lows—only 10%—while production skyrocketed to an all time high. Middle-class families could buy homes, send kids to college, and live on a single income.

Because of progressive taxation and political capital after World War II, the American government made huge investments in the middle class. It sent 7.8 million WWII veterans to college and helped them buy 4.3 million homes through the GI bill. We built the interstate highway system and New Deal dams, bridges, and power plants. We funded NASA and united around the moon landing. It was one of the most prosperous times in US history—we called it the “Golden Age of Capitalism.”

Until the 1980s.

Ronald Reagan dropped the top marginal tax rate from 70% down to 28%, and corporate tax rates fell from 50% down to 30%. Executives began to get paid in equity, with investors and founders getting exorbitantly rich from stock options while the average worker’s wages stagnated. The minimum wage peaked in 1968 then ceased to keep up with inflation. We created a new generation of Carnegies and Rockefellers and Morgans, and now they want to build libraries and universities and call it even. They call any form of taxation a “Soviet Hellscape” as a scare tactic, when in fact we can look back at when America was most taxed and call it a “Capitalist Paradise.”

The problem now, as in the early 1900s, is not that the rich are being flagrant about their money, but that they are earning so much more than everyone else. The answer is not that the rich should be better about their money and build libraries for us all, but that they should pay their workers more. Our next labor movement should demand, not just a higher minimum wage, but also a minimum salary and a minimum equity share. The highest paid person at every company should earn no more than 20x the lowest paid person—the size of the earnings gap in the 1950s and 1960s—and that should go for equity too.

As jobs get automated, we should demand, not just worker ownership of our companies, but collective ownership of the economy. We should tax, not just oil and gas, but all production, so we earn dividends as our companies manufacture cars, spaceships, and even 3D print houses.

But how are we going to create collective ownership of the economy if we can’t even tax the production we already have? Or raise the floor of the workers that already exist? How are we supposed to talk about creating the utopian version of a fully automated workforce that produces everything society needs when we are rapidly heading toward a dystopian techno-feudal future where a few founders own it all and the rest of us work as wage laborers or eventually are automated out of a job?

The rich have no incentive to reduce the wealth gap. They benefit from owning all of our companies and all of our stocks. They benefit from owning our technologies and our satellites. They benefit from owning our space program and our media, our mortgages and our private health insurance. And they use the piles of money they earn from all of the above to ensure no one in Washington so much as whispers the words “tax the rich.” As a result, they bankrupt our country, which has become impoverished to the point that it is $37 trillion in debt, all because they engineer tax breaks for the rich and refuse efforts to raise revenue, even as the rest of us experience cuts to education, healthcare, infrastructure, and R&D.

When the government can no longer fund infrastructure for electric cars or space exploration, scientific advancement and medical cures, technological development and innovation, the private owners do instead and become even richer. Those who own the infrastructure and automation stand to accumulate disproportionate power and benefits so they peddle the myth that we can’t tax them, because we need them to build all this stuff the government can’t build.

“It’s good that America has risk capital,” Doricko says. “Wild and visionary ambition is part of what made our nation great.” And this is the refrain of every wealthy founder ever: We need to be able to get rich, because that’s what makes America so innovative. It is the promise of future million-dollar payouts that inspires entrepreneurs to invent the internet, create autonomous cars, and make it to Mars.

It is not.

We were incredibly innovative during World War II—churning out planes, ships, tanks, and radar at record speed. Defense contractors like Boeing, Lockheed, and Ford won huge government contracts, but profits were taxed at 90-95% as part of wartime regulations. We were incredibly innovative between 1950 and 1970, when income and corporate taxes were at their highest, and a third of the workforce was part of a union. That’s when the government funded satellites and space travel, computing and the internet. That’s when we came out with the color TV, microwave ovens, and jet travel. That’s when we debuted the polio vaccine, completed a successful heart transplant, and invented MRI technology. That’s when we built our first nuclear plant.

No one got rich building these things—yet we built them anyway! Inequality was at its lowest, and quality of life was at an all-time high. Workers didn’t have to worry about money and could spend their workday much higher up Maslow's hierarchy—as scientists and engineers, and with jobs that directly contributed to society. They had strong safety nets, stable jobs, high wages, and affordable education and housing. The companies they worked for—Bell Labs, Xerox, IBM, DuPont, and GE—were stable, well-funded, long-term oriented, and shielded from the short-term profit pressures companies face today. There was little to no venture capital forcing hypergrowth and quick exit events. Stock buybacks were considered manipulation until 1982 so companies invested in R&D instead. Government-funded R&D peaked at nearly 2% of GDP in 1964—compared with 0.62% today—funding the organizations crucial to human progress.

After 1970, we may have created a lot of tech companies, but innovation ceased. Nothing that came after revolutionized our physical living conditions the way airplane travel or telephones did. We shifted from government and corporate labs to startups and consumer tech. We created internet companies and apps, and we created more unicorns and billionaires, but we became much less innovative in the process. Tyler Cowen calls this era the “Great Stagnation”—a period defined by slower productivity growth, stagnation of wages, and sluggish household income growth.

The “Golden Age of Capitalism” had ended.

When, in 2023, Cowen said we might be coming out of the Great Stagnation, he cited the rapid growth of mRNA vaccines, green energy, and ChatGPT as evidence. All three were precipitated by spurts of government funding: $18 billion went into vaccine development during the pandemic through “Operation Warp Speed.” Green energy developments came from grants, tax credits, government-funded battery research and infrastructure development and a $465 million loan to Tesla. If, of that list, we can attribute ChatGPT to a particularly innovative startup founder, even he started out with a capped salary and built his company upon decades of machine learning and transformer research that came out of DARPA, NSF, and military research.

It was capitalism that caused our innovation, yes, but a capitalism that was funded by high taxes and high government spending. It was a capitalism with a high enough floor that a wide middle class had enough purchasing power to keep it going. It was a capitalism where companies were stable enough that we could spend our 40-hour work week contributing to human progress, rather than writing emails and attending meetings and “quiet quitting” from the tedium of it all. If we don’t want to tax the rich today, it’s not because founders will stop innovating companies—they didn’t before. And it’s not because we would be living in a Soviet Hellscape—we weren’t when we taxed the rich. It’s because founders want more money and power for themselves. Plain and simple. And as a result, workers have been cut off from innovating alongside them.

I’m a big fan of Augustus Doricko’s company. I’ve written about Rainmaker before and there is nothing I want more than for technologies like this to exist. I think an America that innovates is incredibly important, and a thriving and robust private sector is an essential part of creating that. But we don’t get there by hanging a $200 million carrot in front of the founder and hoping he chases it somewhere good. We get there with a government rich enough from tax dollars to fund R&D, a populace protected by a high enough floor that they can participate in the economy, and a people well off enough to start companies and contribute to the innovation from here.

Rich tech founders will continue to call any form of taxation “socialism,” and they will continue to say we wouldn’t innovate if they didn’t get rich doing it. Both are untrue, as evidenced by our own country and our own history. Innovation doesn’t require billionaire paydays. It requires taxing them, investing in everyone else, and remembering the Golden Age of Capitalism wasn’t built on greed—it was built on taxes and a minimum wage.

It was built on all of us contributing to innovation, not just a few tech bros hoping to make it rich and own all the capital.

Leave a comment

I hope you’ll join us for further discussion in the comments.

Thank you Austin Tindle, Hara Kumar, and Shoni for editing an early draft and helping workshop my title.

And thank you all for reading,

Elle Griffin

sentences.zh: “他妈的再努力一点。

sentences.zh: 我们建造了州际公路系统和新政水坝、桥梁和发电厂。

sentences.en: We funded NASA and united around the moon landing.
sentences.zh: 我们资助美国宇航局并围绕登月团结起来。

sentences.en: It was one of the most prosperous times in US history—we called it the “Golden Age of Capitalism.”
sentences.zh: 那是美国历史上最繁荣的时代之一——我们称之为“资本主义的黄金时代”。

blocks.html_en: Until the 1980s.

sentences.en: Until the 1980s.
sentences.zh: 直到20世纪80年代。

blocks.html_en: Ronald Reagan dropped the top marginal tax rate from 70% down to 28%, and corporate tax rates fell from 50% down to 30%. Executives began to get paid in equity, with investors and founders getting exorbitantly rich from stock options while the average worker’s wages stagnated. The minimum wage peaked in 1968 then ceased to keep up with inflation. We created a new generation of Carnegies and Rockefellers and Morgans, and now they want to build libraries and universities and call it even. They call any form of taxation a “Soviet Hellscape” as a scare tactic, when in fact we can look back at when America was most taxed and call it a “Capitalist Paradise.”

sentences.en: Ronald Reagan dropped the top marginal tax rate from 70% down to 28%, and corporate tax rates fell from 50% down to 30%.
sentences.zh: 罗纳德·里根将最高边际税率从 70% 降至 28%，公司税率从 50% 降至 30%。

sentences.en: Executives began to get paid in equity, with investors and founders getting exorbitantly rich from stock options while the average worker’s wages stagnated.
sentences.zh: 高管们开始以股权形式获得报酬，投资者和创始人从股票期权中获得巨额财富，而普通工人的工资却停滞不前。

sentences.en: The minimum wage peaked in 1968 then ceased to keep up with inflation.
sentences.zh: 最低工资在 1968 年达到顶峰，随后跟不上通货膨胀。

sentences.en: We created a new generation of Carnegies and Rockefellers and Morgans, and now they want to build libraries and universities and call it even.
sentences.zh: 我们创造了新一代的卡内基、洛克菲勒和摩根，现在他们想要建立图书馆和大学，甚至称之为平局。

sentences.en: They call any form of taxation a “Soviet Hellscape” as a scare tactic, when in fact we can look back at when America was most taxed and call it a “Capitalist Paradise.”
sentences.zh: 他们将任何形式的税收称为“苏联地狱”，作为一种恐吓策略，而事实上，我们可以回顾美国税收最高的时期，并将其称为“资本主义天堂”。

blocks.html_en: The problem now, as in the early 1900s, is not that the rich are being flagrant about their money, but that they are earning so much more than everyone else. The answer is not that the rich should be better about their money and build libraries for us all, but that they should pay their workers more. Our next labor movement should demand, not just a higher minimum wage, but also a minimum salary and a minimum equity share. The highest paid person at every company should earn no more than 20x the lowest paid person—the size of the earnings gap in the 1950s and 1960s—and that should go for equity too.

sentences.en: The problem now, as in the early 1900s, is not that the rich are being flagrant about their money, but that they are earning so much more than everyone else.
sentences.zh: 与 1900 年代初期一样，现在的问题不在于富人公然炫耀自己的钱，而在于他们的收入比其他人高得多。

sentences.en: The answer is not that the rich should be better about their money and build libraries for us all, but that they should pay their workers more.
sentences.zh: 答案不是富人应该更好地花钱并为我们所有人建造图书馆，而是他们应该向工人支付更多工资。

sentences.en: Our next labor movement should demand, not just a higher minimum wage, but also a minimum salary and a minimum equity share.
sentences.zh: 我们的下一次劳工运动不仅要求提高最低工资，还要求最低工资和最低股权份额。

sentences.en: The highest paid person at every company should earn no more than 20x the lowest paid person—the size of the earnings gap in the 1950s and 1960s—and that should go for equity too.
sentences.zh: 每家公司收入最高的人的收入不应超过收入最低的人的 20 倍（相当于 20 世纪 50 年代和 1960 年代的收入差距），这也应该适用于股权。

blocks.html_en: As jobs get automated, we should demand, not just worker ownership of our companies, but collective ownership of the economy. We should tax, not just oil and gas, but all production, so we earn dividends as our companies manufacture cars, spaceships, and even 3D print houses.

sentences.en: As jobs get automated, we should demand, not just worker ownership of our companies, but collective ownership of the economy.
sentences.zh: 随着工作的自动化，我们不仅应该要求工人对公司的所有权，还应该要求对经济的集体所有权。

sentences.en: We should tax, not just oil and gas, but all production, so we earn dividends as our companies manufacture cars, spaceships, and even 3D print houses.
sentences.zh: 我们不仅应该对石油和天然气征税，还应该对所有生产征税，这样我们就能在我们的公司制造汽车、宇宙飞船甚至 3D 打印房屋时获得股息。

blocks.html_en: But how are we going to create collective ownership of the economy if we can’t even tax the production we already have? Or raise the floor of the workers that already exist? How are we supposed to talk about creating the utopian version of a fully automated workforce that produces everything society needs when we are rapidly heading toward a dystopian techno-feudal future where a few founders own it all and the rest of us work as wage laborers or eventually are automated out of a job?

sentences.en: But how are we going to create collective ownership of the economy if we can’t even tax the production we already have?
sentences.zh: 但是，如果我们甚至不能对现有的生产征税，我们如何才能创造经济的集体所有制呢？

sentences.en: Or raise the floor of the workers that already exist?
sentences.zh: 或者提高现有工人的底线？

sentences.en: How are we supposed to talk about creating the utopian version of a fully automated workforce that produces everything society needs when we are rapidly heading toward a dystopian techno-feudal future where a few founders own it all and the rest of us work as wage laborers or eventually are automated out of a job?
sentences.zh: 当我们迅速走向反乌托邦的技术封建未来，少数创始人拥有一切，而我们其他人则充当工资劳动者或最终被自动化失业时，我们该如何谈论创建乌托邦版本的完全自动化的劳动力来生产社会所需的一切呢？

blocks.html_en: The rich have no incentive to reduce the wealth gap. They benefit from owning all of our companies and all of our stocks. They benefit from owning our technologies and our satellites. They benefit from owning our space program and our media, our mortgages and our private health insurance. And they use the piles of money they earn from all of the above to ensure no one in Washington so much as whispers the words “tax the rich.” As a result, they bankrupt our country, which has become impoverished to the point that it is $37 trillion in debt, all because they engineer tax breaks for the rich and refuse efforts to raise revenue, even as the rest of us experience cuts to education, healthcare, infrastructure, and R&D.

sentences.en: The rich have no incentive to reduce the wealth gap.
sentences.zh: 富人没有动力缩小贫富差距。

sentences.en: They benefit from owning all of our companies and all of our stocks.
sentences.zh: 他们受益于拥有我们所有的公司和所有的股票。

sentences.en: They benefit from owning our technologies and our satellites.
sentences.zh: 他们受益于拥有我们的技术和卫星。

sentences.en: They benefit from owning our space program and our media, our mortgages and our private health insurance.
sentences.zh: 他们受益于拥有我们的太空计划、我们的媒体、我们的抵押贷款和我们的私人健康保险。

sentences.en: And they use the piles of money they earn from all of the above to ensure no one in Washington so much as whispers the words “tax the rich.”
sentences.zh: 他们利用从上述所有方面赚取的大量资金来确保华盛顿没有人低声说出“向富人征税”这句话。

sentences.en: As a result, they bankrupt our country, which has become impoverished to the point that it is $37 trillion in debt, all because they engineer tax breaks for the rich and refuse efforts to raise revenue, even as the rest of us experience cuts to education, healthcare, infrastructure, and R&D.
sentences.zh: 结果，他们让我们的国家破产，我们的国家已经陷入贫困，债务达到 37 万亿美元，这一切都是因为他们为富人设计税收减免，拒绝努力增加收入，尽管我们其他人都在经历教育、医疗、基础设施和研发方面的削减。

blocks.html_en: When the government can no longer fund infrastructure for electric cars or space exploration, scientific advancement and medical cures, technological development and innovation, the private owners do instead and become even richer. Those who own the infrastructure and automation stand to accumulate disproportionate power and benefits so they peddle the myth that we can’t tax them, because we need them to build all this stuff the government can’t build.

sentences.en: When the government can no longer fund infrastructure for electric cars or space exploration, scientific advancement and medical cures, technological development and innovation, the private owners do instead and become even richer.
sentences.zh: 当政府无法再为电动汽车或太空探索、科学进步和医疗、技术开发和创新的基础设施提供资金时，私人所有者就会采取行动，并变得更加富有。

sentences.en: Those who own the infrastructure and automation stand to accumulate disproportionate power and benefits so they peddle the myth that we can’t tax them, because we need them to build all this stuff the government can’t build.
sentences.zh: 那些拥有基础设施和自动化的人会积累不成比例的权力和利益，因此他们兜售我们不能向他们征税的神话，因为我们需要他们建造所有这些政府无法建造的东西。

blocks.html_en: “It’s good that America has risk capital,” Doricko says. “Wild and visionary ambition is part of what made our nation great.” And this is the refrain of every wealthy founder ever: We need to be able to get rich, because that’s what makes America so innovative. It is the promise of future million-dollar payouts that inspires entrepreneurs to invent the internet, create autonomous cars, and make it to Mars.

sentences.en: “It’s good that America has risk capital,” Doricko says.
sentences.zh: “美国拥有风险资本是件好事，”多里科说。

sentences.en: “Wild and visionary ambition is part of what made our nation great.”
sentences.zh: “狂野而富有远见的野心是我们国家伟大的一部分。”

sentences.en: And this is the refrain of every wealthy founder ever: We need to be able to get rich, because that’s what makes America so innovative.
sentences.zh: 这是每一位富有的创始人的口头禅：我们需要能够致富，因为这就是美国如此创新的原因。

sentences.en: It is the promise of future million-dollar payouts that inspires entrepreneurs to invent the internet, create autonomous cars, and make it to Mars.
sentences.zh: 正是未来数百万美元支出的承诺激励企业家发明互联网、制造自动驾驶汽车并登上火星。

blocks.html_en: It is not.

sentences.en: It is not.
sentences.zh: 它不是。

blocks.html_en: We were incredibly innovative during World War II—churning out planes, ships, tanks, and radar at record speed. Defense contractors like Boeing, Lockheed, and Ford won huge government contracts, but profits were taxed at 90-95% as part of wartime regulations. We were incredibly innovative between 1950 and 1970, when income and corporate taxes were at their highest, and a third of the workforce was part of a union. That’s when the government funded satellites and space travel, computing and the internet. That’s when we came out with the color TV, microwave ovens, and jet travel. That’s when we debuted the polio vaccine, completed a successful heart transplant, and invented MRI technology. That’s when we built our first nuclear plant.

sentences.en: We were incredibly innovative during World War II—churning out planes, ships, tanks, and radar at record speed.
sentences.zh: 我们在第二次世界大战期间进行了令人难以置信的创新——以创纪录的速度生产飞机、船舶、坦克和雷达。

sentences.en: Defense contractors like Boeing, Lockheed, and Ford won huge government contracts, but profits were taxed at 90-95% as part of wartime regulations.
sentences.zh: 波音、洛克希德和福特等国防承包商赢得了巨额政府合同，但根据战时法规，利润需缴纳 90-95% 的税。

sentences.en: We were incredibly innovative between 1950 and 1970, when income and corporate taxes were at their highest, and a third of the workforce was part of a union.
sentences.zh: 1950 年至 1970 年间，我们进行了令人难以置信的创新，当时所得税和公司税最高，三分之一的劳动力是工会的成员。

sentences.en: That’s when the government funded satellites and space travel, computing and the internet.
sentences.zh: 那时政府资助卫星、太空旅行、计算和互联网。

sentences.en: That’s when we came out with the color TV, microwave ovens, and jet travel.
sentences.zh: 就在那时，我们推出了彩电、微波炉和喷气式飞机。

sentences.en: That’s when we debuted the polio vaccine, completed a successful heart transplant, and invented MRI technology.
sentences.zh: 就在那时，我们首次推出了脊髓灰质炎疫苗，成功完成了心脏移植手术，并发明了核磁共振技术。

sentences.en: That’s when we built our first nuclear plant.
sentences.zh: 那是我们建造第一座核电站的时候。

blocks.html_en: No one got rich building these things—yet we built them anyway! Inequality was at its lowest, and quality of life was at an all-time high. Workers didn’t have to worry about money and could spend their workday much higher up Maslow's hierarchy—as scientists and engineers, and with jobs that directly contributed to society. They had strong safety nets, stable jobs, high wages, and affordable education and housing. The companies they worked for—Bell Labs, Xerox, IBM, DuPont, and GE—were stable, well-funded, long-term oriented, and shielded from the short-term profit pressures companies face today. There was little to no venture capital forcing hypergrowth and quick exit events. Stock buybacks were considered manipulation until 1982 so companies invested in R&D instead. Government-funded R&D peaked at nearly 2% of GDP in 1964—compared with 0.62% today—funding the organizations crucial to human progress.

sentences.en: No one got rich building these things—yet we built them anyway!
sentences.zh: 没有人靠建造这些东西而致富——但我们还是建造了它们！

sentences.en: Inequality was at its lowest, and quality of life was at an all-time high.
sentences.zh: 不平等现象降至最低，生活质量达到历史最高水平。

sentences.en: Workers didn’t have to worry about money and could spend their workday much higher up Maslow's hierarchy—as scientists and engineers, and with jobs that directly contributed to society.
sentences.zh: 工人们不必担心钱的问题，并且可以在马斯洛的层次中占据更高的职位——科学家和工程师，以及直接为社会做出贡献的工作。

sentences.en: They had strong safety nets, stable jobs, high wages, and affordable education and housing.
sentences.zh: 他们拥有强大的安全网、稳定的工作、高工资以及负担得起的教育和住房。

sentences.en: The companies they worked for—Bell Labs, Xerox, IBM, DuPont, and GE—were stable, well-funded, long-term oriented, and shielded from the short-term profit pressures companies face today.
sentences.zh: 他们工作的公司——贝尔实验室、施乐、IBM、杜邦和通用电气——稳定、资金充足、面向长期，并且不受公司今天面临的短期利润压力的影响。

sentences.en: There was little to no venture capital forcing hypergrowth and quick exit events.
sentences.zh: 几乎没有风险资本推动高速增长和快速退出事件。

sentences.en: Stock buybacks were considered manipulation until 1982 so companies invested in R&D instead.
sentences.zh: 1982 年之前，股票回购一直被认为是操纵行为，因此公司转而投资于研发。

sentences.en: Government-funded R&D peaked at nearly 2% of GDP in 1964—compared with 0.62% today—funding the organizations crucial to human progress.
sentences.zh: 政府资助的研发在 1964 年达到顶峰，占 GDP 的近 2%（而今天为 0.62%），为对人类进步至关重要的组织提供资金。

blocks.html_en: After 1970, we may have created a lot of tech companies, but innovation ceased. Nothing that came after revolutionized our physical living conditions the way airplane travel or telephones did. We shifted from government and corporate labs to startups and consumer tech. We created internet companies and apps, and we created more unicorns and billionaires, but we became much less innovative in the process. Tyler Cowen calls this era the “Great Stagnation”—a period defined by slower productivity growth, stagnation of wages, and sluggish household income growth.

sentences.en: After 1970, we may have created a lot of tech companies, but innovation ceased.
sentences.zh: 1970 年后，我们可能创建了很多科技公司，但创新却停止了。

sentences.en: Nothing that came after revolutionized our physical living conditions the way airplane travel or telephones did.
sentences.zh: 此后发生的一切都没有像飞机旅行或电话那样彻底改变我们的物质生活条件。

sentences.en: We shifted from government and corporate labs to startups and consumer tech.
sentences.zh: 我们从政府和企业实验室转向初创公司和消费技术。

sentences.en: We created internet companies and apps, and we created more unicorns and billionaires, but we became much less innovative in the process.
sentences.zh: 我们创建了互联网公司和应用程序，创建了更多的独角兽和亿万富翁，但在这个过程中我们的创新性却大大降低了。

sentences.en: Tyler Cowen calls this era the “Great Stagnation”—a period defined by slower productivity growth, stagnation of wages, and sluggish household income growth.
sentences.zh: 泰勒·考恩 (Tyler Cowen) 将这个时代称为“大停滞”，即生产力增长放缓、工资停滞和家庭收入增长缓慢的时期。

blocks.html_en: The “Golden Age of Capitalism” had ended.

sentences.en: The “Golden Age of Capitalism” had ended.
sentences.zh: “资本主义的黄金时代”已经结束。

blocks.html_en: When, in 2023, Cowen said we might be coming out of the Great Stagnation, he cited the rapid growth of mRNA vaccines, green energy, and ChatGPT as evidence. All three were precipitated by spurts of government funding: $18 billion went into vaccine development during the pandemic through “Operation Warp Speed.” Green energy developments came from grants, tax credits, government-funded battery research and infrastructure development and a $465 million loan to Tesla. If, of that list, we can attribute ChatGPT to a particularly innovative startup founder, even he started out with a capped salary and built his company upon decades of machine learning and transformer research that came out of DARPA, NSF, and military research.

sentences.en: When, in 2023, Cowen said we might be coming out of the Great Stagnation, he cited the rapid growth of mRNA vaccines, green energy, and ChatGPT as evidence.
sentences.zh: 当考恩在 2023 年表示我们可能会走出大停滞时，他引用了 mRNA 疫苗、绿色能源和 ChatGPT 的快速增长作为证据。

sentences.en: All three were precipitated by spurts of government funding: $18 billion went into vaccine development during the pandemic through “Operation Warp Speed.”
sentences.zh: 这三件事都是由政府资金激增促成的：大流行期间，通过“曲速行动”投入了 180 亿美元用于疫苗开发。

sentences.en: Green energy developments came from grants, tax credits, government-funded battery research and infrastructure development and a $465 million loan to Tesla.
sentences.zh: 绿色能源的发展来自赠款、税收抵免、政府资助的电池研究和基础设施开发以及向特斯拉提供的 4.65 亿美元贷款。

sentences.en: If, of that list, we can attribute ChatGPT to a particularly innovative startup founder, even he started out with a capped salary and built his company upon decades of machine learning and transformer research that came out of DARPA, NSF, and military research.
sentences.zh: 如果说，在这份名单中，我们可以将 ChatGPT 归功于一位特别具有创新精神的初创公司创始人，即使他一开始的工资也有上限，但他的公司是建立在 DARPA、NSF 和军事研究数十年的机器学习和变压器研究基础上的。

blocks.html_en: It was capitalism that caused our innovation, yes, but a capitalism that was funded by high taxes and high government spending. It was a capitalism with a high enough floor that a wide middle class had enough purchasing power to keep it going. It was a capitalism where companies were stable enough that we could spend our 40-hour work week contributing to human progress, rather than writing emails and attending meetings and “quiet quitting” from the tedium of it all. If we don’t want to tax the rich today, it’s not because founders will stop innovating companies—they didn’t before. And it’s not because we would be living in a Soviet Hellscape—we weren’t when we taxed the rich. It’s because founders want more money and power for themselves. Plain and simple. And as a result, workers have been cut off from innovating alongside them.

sentences.en: It was capitalism that caused our innovation, yes, but a capitalism that was funded by high taxes and high government spending.
sentences.zh: 是的，是资本主义引发了我们的创新，但这种资本主义是由高税收和高政府支出资助的。

sentences.en: It was a capitalism with a high enough floor that a wide middle class had enough purchasing power to keep it going.
sentences.zh: 这是一种具有足够高水平的资本主义，广大中产阶级有足够的购买力来维持它的发展。

sentences.en: It was a capitalism where companies were stable enough that we could spend our 40-hour work week contributing to human progress, rather than writing emails and attending meetings and “quiet quitting” from the tedium of it all.
sentences.zh: 这是一种资本主义，公司足够稳定，我们可以每周工作 40 小时，为人类进步做出贡献，而不是写电子邮件、参加会议，然后“安静地退出”这一切乏味的事情。

sentences.en: If we don’t want to tax the rich today, it’s not because founders will stop innovating companies—they didn’t before.
sentences.zh: 如果我们今天不想对富人征税，这并不是因为创始人会停止创新公司——他们以前没有这样做。

sentences.en: And it’s not because we would be living in a Soviet Hellscape—we weren’t when we taxed the rich.
sentences.zh: 这并不是因为我们会生活在苏联的地狱般的景象中——当我们向富人征税时，我们就不会生活在苏联的地狱里了。

sentences.en: It’s because founders want more money and power for themselves.
sentences.zh: 这是因为创始人想要为自己争取更多的金钱和权力。

sentences.en: Plain and simple.
sentences.zh: 简单明了。

sentences.en: And as a result, workers have been cut off from innovating alongside them.
sentences.zh: 结果，工人们无法与他们一起创新。

blocks.html_en: I’m a big fan of Augustus Doricko’s company. I’ve written about Rainmaker before and there is nothing I want more than for technologies like this to exist. I think an America that innovates is incredibly important, and a thriving and robust private sector is an essential part of creating that. But we don’t get there by hanging a $200 million carrot in front of the founder and hoping he chases it somewhere good. We get there with a government rich enough from tax dollars to fund R&D, a populace protected by a high enough floor that they can participate in the economy, and a people well off enough to start companies and contribute to the innovation from here.

sentences.en: I’m a big fan of Augustus Doricko’s company.
sentences.zh: 我是 Augustus Doricko 公司的忠实粉丝。

sentences.en: I’ve written about Rainmaker before and there is nothing I want more than for technologies like this to exist.
sentences.zh: 我之前写过有关造雨人的文章，我最希望看到这样的技术存在。

sentences.en: I think an America that innovates is incredibly important, and a thriving and robust private sector is an essential part of creating that.
sentences.zh: 我认为创新的美国非常重要，而繁荣而强大的私营部门是创造创新的重要组成部分。

sentences.en: But we don’t get there by hanging a $200 million carrot in front of the founder and hoping he chases it somewhere good.
sentences.zh: 但我们不会把价值 2 亿美元的胡萝卜挂在创始人面前，希​​望他把它追逐到好的地方来实现这一目标。

sentences.en: We get there with a government rich enough from tax dollars to fund R&D, a populace protected by a high enough floor that they can participate in the economy, and a people well off enough to start companies and contribute to the innovation from here.
sentences.zh: 我们到达那里时，政府有足够的税收来资助研发，民众受到足够高的保护，可以参与经济，人民也足够富裕，可以在这里创办公司并为创新做出贡献。

blocks.html_en: Rich tech founders will continue to call any form of taxation “socialism,” and they will continue to say we wouldn’t innovate if they didn’t get rich doing it. Both are untrue, as evidenced by our own country and our own history. Innovation doesn’t require billionaire paydays. It requires taxing them, investing in everyone else, and remembering the Golden Age of Capitalism wasn’t built on greed—it was built on taxes and a minimum wage.

sentences.en: Rich tech founders will continue to call any form of taxation “socialism,” and they will continue to say we wouldn’t innovate if they didn’t get rich doing it.
sentences.zh: 富有的科技创始人将继续将任何形式的税收称为“社会主义”，他们将继续说，如果他们不这样做致富，我们就不会创新。

sentences.en: Both are untrue, as evidenced by our own country and our own history.
sentences.zh: 正如我们自己的国家和我们自己的历史所证明的那样，两者都是不真实的。

sentences.en: Innovation doesn’t require billionaire paydays.
sentences.zh: 创新不需要亿万富翁的发薪日。

sentences.en: It requires taxing them, investing in everyone else, and remembering the Golden Age of Capitalism wasn’t built on greed—it was built on taxes and a minimum wage.
sentences.zh: 它需要对他们征税，对其他所有人进行投资，并记住资本主义的黄金时代不是建立在贪婪之上的，而是建立在税收和最低工资之上的。

blocks.html_en: It was built on all of us contributing to innovation, not just a few tech bros hoping to make it rich and own all the capital.

sentences.en: It was built on all of us contributing to innovation, not just a few tech bros hoping to make it rich and own all the capital.
sentences.zh: 它建立在我们所有人为创新做出贡献的基础上，而不仅仅是少数希望致富并拥有所有资本的科技兄弟。

blocks.html_en: Leave a comment

sentences.en: Leave a comment
sentences.zh: 发表评论

blocks.html_en: I hope you’ll join us for further discussion in the comments.

sentences.en: I hope you’ll join us for further discussion in the comments.
sentences.zh: 希望您能在评论中加入我们进一步讨论。

blocks.html_en: Thank you Austin Tindle, Hara Kumar, and Shoni for editing an early draft and helping workshop my title.

sentences.en: Thank you Austin Tindle, Hara Kumar, and Shoni for editing an early draft and helping workshop my title.
sentences.zh: 感谢 Austin Tindle、Hara Kumar 和 Shoni 编辑了早期草稿并帮助改进了我的标题。

blocks.html_en: And thank you all for reading,

sentences.en: And thank you all for reading,
sentences.zh: 感谢大家的阅读，

blocks.html_en: Elle Griffin

sentences.en: Elle Griffin
sentences.zh: 艾丽·格里芬

content_html_zh:
这篇文章是我公开撰写的《我们应该拥有经济》一书的一部分。 读者仍然可以支持该项目，并在项目出售时分享利润👇🏻

了解更多

美国的亿万富翁会把任何东西称为“社会主义”——只要它能让他们不用纳税。

他们鹦鹉学舌地认为我们需要亿万富翁，因为百万美元的承诺激励他们建造互联网、自动驾驶汽车和火箭。

“你想避免生活在苏联地狱吗？” Rainmaker 首席执行官 Augustus Doricko 最近在 X 上向创始人询问。 “他妈的再努力一点。 别再浪费那么多钱了，大多数美国人都会跪下来向上帝哭诉感谢。 积极确保全国每个人都能从你的劳动中获得一部分回报，否则他们就会从你手中夺走它。”

他说，如果创始人对他们的钱更加感激，并更加努力地工作，而不是展示他们丰富的生活方式，他们就不会引起中产阶级的愤怒，他们的收入少了1000倍，永远买不起房子，现在对他们的公司创始人感到义愤填膺，并呼吁“向富人征税”。

相反，多里科钦佩那些辛勤工作、建设公共工程的“老资本家”。 如果创始人像他们一样，我们就不会呼吁“社会主义”。

恕我直言，这是荒谬的。 卡内基、洛克菲勒和摩根家族的收入是普通工人的五万多倍。 他们可能建立了漂亮的公共图书馆和大学，但工人们并不感激——他们举行罢工和抗议。 《公平劳动标准法》的颁布使愤怒达到顶峰：1938 年，资本家被迫向雇员支付最低工资，到 1940 年代，最高边际税率提高到 94%。

它奏效了——非常成功。

资本家被迫向工人支付更高的工资，因此他们的收入必然减少。 为了避免缴纳高额所得税，他们向公司和工人投资了更多的钱。 工人们的收入更高，有能力购买这些公司生产的产品。 从 20 世纪 40 年代到 1970 年代，美国的经济是有史以来最平等的。 1% 顶层的人的收入份额降至历史低点——只有 10%——而产量却飙升至历史新高。 中产阶级家庭可以购买房屋，送孩子上大学，并靠单一收入生活。

由于累进税制和二战后的政治资本，美国政府对中产阶级进行了大量投资。 它帮助 780 万二战老兵上大学，并通过退伍军人法案帮助他们购买了 430 万套住房。 我们建造了州际公路系统和新政水坝、桥梁和发电厂。 我们资助美国宇航局并围绕登月团结起来。 那是美国历史上最繁荣的时代之一——我们称之为“资本主义的黄金时代”。

直到20世纪80年代。

罗纳德·里根将最高边际税率从 70% 降至 28%，公司税率从 50% 降至 30%。 高管们开始以股权形式获得报酬，投资者和创始人从股票期权中获得巨额财富，而普通工人的工资却停滞不前。 最低工资在 1968 年达到顶峰，随后跟不上通货膨胀。 我们创造了新一代的卡内基、洛克菲勒和摩根，现在他们想要建立图书馆和大学，甚至称之为平局。 他们将任何形式的税收称为“苏联地狱”，作为一种恐吓策略，而事实上，我们可以回顾美国税收最高的时期，并将其称为“资本主义天堂”。

与 1900 年代初期一样，现在的问题不在于富人公然炫耀自己的钱，而在于他们的收入比其他人高得多。 答案不是富人应该更好地花钱并为我们所有人建造图书馆，而是他们应该向工人支付更多工资。 我们的下一次劳工运动不仅要求提高最低工资，还要求最低工资和最低股权份额。 每家公司收入最高的人的收入不应超过收入最低的人的 20 倍（相当于 20 世纪 50 年代和 1960 年代的收入差距），这也应该适用于股权。

随着工作的自动化，我们不仅应该要求工人对公司的所有权，还应该要求对经济的集体所有权。 我们不仅应该对石油和天然气征税，还应该对所有生产征税，这样我们就能在我们的公司制造汽车、宇宙飞船甚至 3D 打印房屋时获得股息。

但是，如果我们甚至不能对现有的生产征税，我们如何才能创造经济的集体所有制呢？ 或者提高现有工人的底线？ 当我们迅速走向反乌托邦的技术封建未来，少数创始人拥有一切，而我们其他人则充当工资劳动者或最终被自动化失业时，我们该如何谈论创建乌托邦版本的完全自动化的劳动力来生产社会所需的一切呢？

富人没有动力缩小贫富差距。 他们受益于拥有我们所有的公司和所有的股票。 他们受益于拥有我们的技术和卫星。 他们受益于拥有我们的太空计划、我们的媒体、我们的抵押贷款和我们的私人健康保险。 他们利用从上述所有方面赚取的大量资金来确保华盛顿没有人低声说出“向富人征税”这句话。 结果，他们让我们的国家破产，我们的国家已经陷入贫困，债务达到 37 万亿美元，这一切都是因为他们为富人设计税收减免，拒绝努力增加收入，尽管我们其他人都在经历教育、医疗、基础设施和研发方面的削减。

当政府无法再为电动汽车或太空探索、科学进步和医疗、技术开发和创新的基础设施提供资金时，私人所有者就会采取行动，并变得更加富有。 那些拥有基础设施和自动化的人会积累不成比例的权力和利益，因此他们兜售我们不能向他们征税的神话，因为我们需要他们建造所有这些政府无法建造的东西。

“美国拥有风险资本是件好事，”多里科说。 “狂野而富有远见的野心是我们国家伟大的一部分。” 这是每一位富有的创始人的口头禅：我们需要能够致富，因为这就是美国如此创新的原因。 正是未来数百万美元支出的承诺激励企业家发明互联网、制造自动驾驶汽车并登上火星。

它不是。

我们在第二次世界大战期间进行了令人难以置信的创新——以创纪录的速度生产飞机、船舶、坦克和雷达。 波音、洛克希德和福特等国防承包商赢得了巨额政府合同，但根据战时法规，利润需缴纳 90-95% 的税。 1950 年至 1970 年间，我们进行了令人难以置信的创新，当时所得税和公司税最高，三分之一的劳动力是工会的成员。 那时政府资助卫星、太空旅行、计算和互联网。 就在那时，我们推出了彩电、微波炉和喷气式飞机。 就在那时，我们首次推出了脊髓灰质炎疫苗，成功完成了心脏移植手术，并发明了核磁共振技术。 那是我们建造第一座核电站的时候。

没有人靠建造这些东西而致富——但我们还是建造了它们！ 不平等现象降至最低，生活质量达到历史最高水平。 工人们不必担心钱的问题，并且可以在马斯洛的层次中占据更高的职位——科学家和工程师，以及直接为社会做出贡献的工作。 他们拥有强大的安全网、稳定的工作、高工资以及负担得起的教育和住房。 他们工作的公司——贝尔实验室、施乐、IBM、杜邦和通用电气——稳定、资金充足、面向长期，并且不受公司今天面临的短期利润压力的影响。 几乎没有风险资本推动高速增长和快速退出事件。 1982 年之前，股票回购一直被认为是操纵行为，因此公司转而投资于研发。 政府资助的研发在 1964 年达到顶峰，占 GDP 的近 2%（而今天为 0.62%），为对人类进步至关重要的组织提供资金。

1970 年后，我们可能创建了很多科技公司，但创新却停止了。 此后发生的一切都没有像飞机旅行或电话那样彻底改变我们的物质生活条件。 我们从政府和企业实验室转向初创公司和消费技术。 我们创建了互联网公司和应用程序，创建了更多的独角兽和亿万富翁，但在这个过程中我们的创新性却大大降低了。 泰勒·考恩 (Tyler Cowen) 将这个时代称为“大停滞”，即生产力增长放缓、工资停滞和家庭收入增长缓慢的时期。

“资本主义的黄金时代”已经结束。

当考恩在 2023 年表示我们可能会走出大停滞时，他引用了 mRNA 疫苗、绿色能源和 ChatGPT 的快速增长作为证据。 这三件事都是由政府资金激增促成的：大流行期间，通过“曲速行动”投入了 180 亿美元用于疫苗开发。 绿色能源的发展来自赠款、税收抵免、政府资助的电池研究和基础设施开发以及向特斯拉提供的 4.65 亿美元贷款。 如果说，在这份名单中，我们可以将 ChatGPT 归功于一位特别具有创新精神的初创公司创始人，即使他一开始的工资也有上限，但他的公司是建立在 DARPA、NSF 和军事研究数十年的机器学习和变压器研究基础上的。

是的，是资本主义引发了我们的创新，但这种资本主义是由高税收和高政府支出资助的。 这是一种具有足够高水平的资本主义，广大中产阶级有足够的购买力来维持它的发展。 这是一种资本主义，公司足够稳定，我们可以每周工作 40 小时，为人类进步做出贡献，而不是写电子邮件、参加会议，然后“安静地退出”这一切乏味的事情。 如果我们今天不想对富人征税，这并不是因为创始人会停止创新公司——他们以前没有这样做。 这并不是因为我们会生活在苏联的地狱般的景象中——当我们向富人征税时，我们就不会生活在苏联的地狱里了。 这是因为创始人想要为自己争取更多的金钱和权力。 简单明了。 结果，工人们无法与他们一起创新。

我是 Augustus Doricko 公司的忠实粉丝。 我之前写过有关造雨人的文章，我最希望看到这样的技术存在。 我认为创新的美国非常重要，而繁荣而强大的私营部门是创造创新的重要组成部分。 但我们不会把价值 2 亿美元的胡萝卜挂在创始人面前，希​​望他把它追逐到好的地方来实现这一目标。 我们到达那里时，政府有足够的税收来资助研发，民众受到足够高的保护，可以参与经济，人民也足够富裕，可以在这里创办公司并为创新做出贡献。

富有的科技创始人将继续将任何形式的税收称为“社会主义”，他们将继续说，如果他们不这样做致富，我们就不会创新。 正如我们自己的国家和我们自己的历史所证明的那样，两者都是不真实的。 创新不需要亿万富翁的发薪日。 它需要对他们征税，对其他所有人进行投资，并记住资本主义的黄金时代不是建立在贪婪之上的，而是建立在税收和最低工资之上的。

它建立在我们所有人为创新做出贡献的基础上，而不仅仅是少数希望致富并拥有所有资本的科技兄弟。

发表评论

希望您能在评论中加入我们进一步讨论。

感谢 Austin Tindle、Hara Kumar 和 Shoni 编辑了早期草稿并帮助改进了我的标题。

感谢大家的阅读，

艾丽·格里芬
